meta.title: Pain Management Prior Auth: Avoiding the Top 5 Denial Reasons
meta.description: Why epidural, facet, and RFA authorizations get denied — and how to prevent denials with better documentation and LOMN preparation.

hero.h1: Pain Management Prior Auth: Avoiding the Top 5 Denial Reasons
hero.subhead: 15-25% of pain procedure authorizations are denied on first submission. Most denials are preventable with proper documentation.

content.h2: The Prior Auth Burden in Pain Management
content.body:
Interventional pain practices submit 50-100 prior authorization requests per month. Each request consumes 15-30 minutes of staff time. When authorizations are denied, appeals add another 30-60 minutes — plus weeks of delay for patients in pain.

The administrative cost is substantial: 25-50 staff hours per month on prior auth alone. But the real cost is denials that don't get appealed and authorizations that expire before patients can be scheduled.

Denial rates vary by procedure and payer, but 15-25% first-submission denial rates are common for pain procedures. The good news: most denials are preventable with better upfront documentation.

content.h2: Denial Reason #1: Insufficient Conservative Care Documentation
content.body:
**The denial:** 'Patient has not completed adequate conservative treatment prior to interventional procedure.'

**Why it happens:**
Payers require evidence of conservative care before approving injections or ablations. Physical therapy, medication trials, home exercise programs — these need to be documented before interventional treatment.

The problem isn't that patients haven't done conservative care. It's that the documentation doesn't make it into the authorization request. PT notes are in a separate system. Medication trials are buried in progress notes. The auth request says 'failed conservative care' without specifics.

**How to prevent it:**

1. **Track conservative care from first visit.** When a patient starts PT, log it. When you prescribe NSAIDs, muscle relaxants, or neuropathic agents, document expected duration and outcome.

2. **Create a conservative care summary.** Before submitting auth, compile: PT visits (dates, duration, outcomes), medication trials (drug, dose, duration, reason for discontinuation), home exercise compliance, activity modifications.

3. **Include specifics in LOMN.** Not 'patient tried PT' but 'patient completed 8 sessions of physical therapy from 1/15/25 to 3/10/25 with minimal improvement in pain scores (7/10 to 6/10).'

4. **Document why conservative care has failed.** Payers want to know the patient gave it a real try. 'PT discontinued due to worsening symptoms' is better than 'PT completed.'

content.h2: Denial Reason #2: Imaging Doesn't Support Requested Procedure
content.body:
**The denial:** 'MRI findings do not correlate with requested injection level' or 'imaging does not demonstrate pathology requiring intervention.'

**Why it happens:**
Payers match imaging findings to procedure requests. If you're requesting L4-5 epidural but the MRI shows pathology at L5-S1, they'll deny. If imaging shows 'mild degenerative changes' and you're requesting RFA, they'll question medical necessity.

**How to prevent it:**

1. **Match procedure level to imaging findings.** Review MRI/CT before submitting. Ensure requested levels correlate with documented pathology.

2. **Describe imaging findings specifically.** Not 'MRI shows herniation' but 'MRI demonstrates L4-5 left paracentral disc protrusion with effacement of the left L5 nerve root, correlating with patient's left L5 radiculopathy.'

3. **Address 'mild' findings proactively.** If imaging shows mild pathology but patient has significant symptoms, explain the correlation. 'Despite mild imaging findings, patient demonstrates clinical signs consistent with facet-mediated pain including paraspinal tenderness and pain with extension.'

4. **Include comparison to prior imaging.** 'Compared to MRI of 6/2024, current imaging demonstrates interval progression of L5-S1 stenosis.'

content.h2: Denial Reason #3: Step Therapy Requirements Not Met
content.body:
**The denial:** 'Patient must complete [X procedure] before [Y procedure] can be authorized.'

**Why it happens:**
Many payers require procedural step therapy. Common requirements:
• ESI before RFA consideration
• Medial branch blocks before RFA
• Diagnostic injection before therapeutic injection at same level
• Conservative injection series before spinal cord stimulator

**How to prevent it:**

1. **Know payer-specific requirements.** Different payers have different step therapy rules. Create a reference sheet for your top payers.

2. **Document diagnostic blocks properly.** Medial branch blocks require documented pain relief (typically 80%+) to justify RFA. Include pre-block and post-block pain scores.

3. **Track procedure history in auth request.** 'Patient has completed two epidural steroid injections (3/1/25, 4/15/25) with temporary relief lasting 4-6 weeks. RFA requested for longer-term relief.'

4. **Explain exceptions when applicable.** Some patients can't complete step therapy due to contraindications. Document why and request exception.

**The denial:** 'Clinical information submitted does not support medical necessity' or 'diagnosis codes do not support requested procedure.'

**Why it happens:**
The auth request is missing critical clinical information. Pain scores absent. Functional limitations not described. Diagnosis codes don't match the procedure requested.

**How to prevent it:**

1. **Include current pain assessment.** Pain score, location, quality, radiation pattern, aggravating/alleviating factors.

2. **Document functional impact.** Not 'patient has back pain' but 'patient unable to sit for more than 20 minutes, limiting ability to work as accountant. Unable to lift grandchildren. Sleep disrupted 3-4 nights per week.'

3. **Match diagnosis codes to procedure.** If requesting lumbar ESI, diagnosis should be lumbar radiculopathy or herniated disc — not just 'low back pain.' Review ICD-10 codes before submission.

4. **Include physical exam findings.** Positive straight leg raise, focal weakness, sensory changes, reflex abnormalities — objective findings supporting the diagnosis.

content.h2: Denial Reason #5: Authorization Expired or Procedure Limit Reached
content.body:
**The denial:** 'Procedure exceeds annual/lifetime limit' or 'authorization has expired.'

**Why it happens:**
Patient wasn't scheduled within auth window, or they've exceeded payer's procedure limits (e.g., 3 ESIs per year, 2 RFA per region per year).

**How to prevent it:**

1. **Track authorization expiration dates.** Most auths expire in 30-90 days. Calendar reminders or system alerts before expiration.

2. **Schedule immediately upon approval.** Don't let auths sit. When approval comes in, schedule the procedure.

3. **Know payer procedure limits.** Track how many procedures patient has had per region per year. Request auth for different level/region if limits reached.

4. **Request reauthorization proactively.** If patient can't be scheduled before auth expires, request extension or reauthorization before it lapses.

Most denials can be prevented with a well-prepared Letter of Medical Necessity. The LOMN should address all common denial reasons before they happen.

**Essential LOMN components:**

1. **Patient identification and procedure requested**

2. **Diagnosis with ICD-10 codes** — specific codes matching the procedure

3. **Clinical presentation** — pain location, duration, severity, functional impact

4. **Physical exam findings** — objective evidence supporting diagnosis

5. **Imaging findings** — specific findings at specific levels, correlated to symptoms

6. **Conservative care history** — dates, duration, outcomes for each modality

7. **Prior procedure history** — dates, outcomes, duration of relief

8. **Medical necessity statement** — why this procedure is appropriate now

**Time savings with templates:**

A well-designed LOMN template pulls patient data automatically — conservative care history, imaging reports, prior procedures, diagnosis codes. Provider reviews, adds clinical judgment, and signs.

Template-based LOMN: 5-10 minutes.
Manual LOMN from scratch: 20-45 minutes.

clinIQ includes LOMN templates for common pain procedures that auto-populate from patient records.

content.h2: When Denials Happen: The Appeal Process
content.body:
Even with perfect documentation, some auths will be denied. A systematic appeal process recovers revenue that would otherwise be lost.

**Appeal success rates by denial reason:**
• Documentation deficiency: 60-70% overturn rate with complete resubmission
• Medical necessity: 40-50% overturn rate with peer-to-peer
• Step therapy: 30-40% overturn rate with exception documentation
• Procedure limits: Low overturn rate unless error in payer records

**Peer-to-peer review:**

For medical necessity denials, request peer-to-peer review. This is a conversation between your physician and the payer's medical director. Come prepared with:
• Complete clinical documentation
• Specific responses to denial reasons
• Published guidelines supporting the procedure
• Patient's current functional status and treatment goals

**Track appeal outcomes:**

Document which payers deny most frequently, which denial reasons are most common, and which are successfully appealed. This informs both your documentation improvement and your payer contracting conversations.
